1: A theme: “In the days when the judges ruled Israel, there was a famine in the land.” The narrative begins with hunger inside the promised land — covenant curse enacted (Deuteronomy 28:23-24). The famine that drives Elimelech’s family to Moab is the same pattern as Jacob’s family driven to Egypt: the covenant people in extremity, the inheritance temporarily inaccessible. Christ enters a world under covenant curse — “the whole creation has been groaning” (Romans 8:22) — as the one who will turn famine into fullness.
2: A theme: Elimelech (“my God is king”) leaves Bethlehem (“house of bread”) because there is no bread. His sons are Mahlon (“sickness”) and Chilion (“destruction”). The names read as a theological commentary: the one who confesses God as king departs from the house of bread, his sons named for the consequences of covenant crisis. The gospel reversal is total: the true King returns to Bethlehem (Matthew 2:1), the house of bread becomes the birthplace of the Bread of Life (John 6:35).
3: A theme: Elimelech dies, leaving Naomi widowed in a foreign land. Every death in Ruth contributes to the void the kinsman-redeemer must fill. Christ is the one who abolishes death (2 Timothy 1:10) and promises that the bereaved will be comforted (Matthew 5:4) — not as cold theology but as the reversal of precisely this kind of irreversible loss.
4: A theme: the sons take Moabite wives. This marriage across ethnic lines would have been scandalous (Deuteronomy 23:3 excluded Moabites). Yet Matthew and Luke both name Ruth explicitly in the genealogy of Christ (Matthew 1:5; Luke 3:32). The messianic line threads through the supposedly excluded: Tamar, Rahab, Ruth, Bathsheba. The inclusion of the unexpected is not oversight — it is the grammar of grace, establishing that Christ comes for all nations.
5: A theme: both sons die — triple bereavement in a foreign land. No male heir, no land, no future by any human calculation. This is the nadir from which only a redeemer can produce restoration. Christ enters the nadir of human covenant failure: “while we were still sinners, Christ died for us” (Romans 5:8). Redemption begins from the lowest point, not after partial recovery.
6: A revelation of God: Naomi hears that “the LORD had visited his people and given them food.” The word pāqad (visited) is the Exodus vocabulary of divine attendance to the afflicted (Genesis 50:24-25; Exodus 3:16). Zechariah’s prophecy at John’s birth uses this exact vocabulary: “Blessed be the Lord God of Israel, for he has visited and redeemed his people” (Luke 1:68). The visitation that brings bread to Bethlehem anticipates the Visitor who becomes the Bread.
7: A theme: Naomi sets out to return from Moab to Bethlehem with both daughters-in-law. Ruth’s journey from Moab to Israel’s covenant community is the individual form of what Isaiah prophesied at cosmic scale: the nations streaming to Zion (Isaiah 2:2-3). The Gentile’s personal covenant entry points toward the great eschatological gathering of all nations.
8: A theme: Naomi urges her daughters-in-law to return to their mothers’ houses, praying YHWH would show them hesed as they had shown hesed. She releases them from obligation. Christ similarly does not coerce discipleship: “Will you also go away?” (John 6:67). Those who remain do so not because they must but because, with Peter, they have nowhere else to go that gives life (John 6:68).
9: A theme: Naomi asks that YHWH grant them mānôaḥ — a settled resting place — in the house of a new husband. This is what she cannot provide; it requires someone outside her household to supply. The rest that no human circumstances can provide is what Christ promises: “Come to me, all who labor and are heavy laden, and I will give you rest” (Matthew 11:28). Naomi’s prayer points beyond itself to the one who is himself the rest of his people.
10: A theme: both women refuse to leave — weeping, loyal. Ruth’s persistence through Naomi’s three attempts to send her away (Ruth 1:8, 11, 15) is the persistence of genuine covenant belonging. The NT makes the same point about the security of those who belong to Christ: they cannot ultimately be argued or pressured away (John 10:29: “no one can snatch them out of the Father’s hand”).
11: A theme: Naomi’s levirate logic — “Do I still have sons in my womb?” — exposes the limits of human legal provision. The kinsman-redeemer institution requires a living male relative; Naomi has none. The redeemer must come from outside the apparent options. The incarnation similarly comes from beyond what human biological or legal structures can provide: “born of a woman” (Galatians 4:4), not from any human father’s initiative.
12: A theme: “I am too old to have a husband. Even if I said I had hope...” The repetition of impossibility strengthens the typological pattern: what the law requires, human capacity cannot supply. Sarah’s laughter, Hannah’s barrenness, Elizabeth’s old age — each case sharpens the pattern that climaxes in the virgin birth, where YHWH provides what no human mechanism can produce.
13: A revelation of God: “the LORD’s hand has gone out against me.” Naomi reads her own affliction as divine action. The same God who afflicts is the one who can restore — and only YHWH can reverse what YHWH has done. The gospel promises this exact reversal: the one whose law condemns (YHWH’s hand against us) provides the redeemer (YHWH’s own Son for us).
14: A shadow: Orpah kisses Naomi and returns; Ruth clings (dābaq) to her. The same covenant-bond verb used for marriage (Genesis 2:24) and Israel’s attachment to YHWH (Deuteronomy 10:20) governs Ruth’s choice. The two daughters represent the two responses to covenant invitation: returning to the familiar, or clinging to the people and God of the promise. The NT knows the same fork: many disciples turned back (John 6:66) while the Twelve clung.
15: A theme: Naomi frames the alternative as a return to “her people and her gods.” The choice is not between Naomi and loneliness; it is between YHWH and Chemosh. False gods do not simply fade — they call back. Christ makes the same exclusive claim: “No one can serve two masters” (Matthew 6:24). Ruth’s refusal is a full conversion, not a cultural accommodation.
16: A direct type: “Your people shall be my people, and your God my God.” Ruth’s declaration is the OT’s most explicit voluntary covenant-entry by a Gentile. She identifies with the covenant people and their God by an act of personal commitment, not ethnic birth. Paul’s argument that the true children of Abraham are those who share his faith (Romans 4:11-12; Galatians 3:7) has its OT paradigm case in Ruth. She is the grammar of grace: Gentile faith-entry into the covenant.
17: A shadow: “Where you die, I will die, and there I will be buried.” Ruth’s commitment extends to death and burial — full solidarity with Naomi’s mortality. This death-solidarity anticipates Christ’s solidarity with humanity in death: “since the children share in flesh and blood, he himself likewise partook of the same things, that through death he might destroy the one who has the power of death” (Hebrews 2:14). The redeemer must enter death to conquer it.
18: A theme: when Naomi saw Ruth was mit’ammeṣet — firmly resolved — she stopped arguing. The resolve of genuine faith is not shaken by rational objections. Those who belong to Christ are similarly described as those who cannot be snatched from the Father’s hand (John 10:29) — held not by their own grip but by the Spirit’s unbreakable work in them.
19: A theme: they arrive in Bethlehem and the whole city is stirred. Bethlehem — house of bread — is the place where bread returns: first in the harvest, then in Boaz’s son, and ultimately in the birth of the Bread of Life (John 6:51). Providence has directed the journey to exactly the right city, as it will when a census brings Mary and Joseph to the same place eight centuries later (Luke 2:4-7).
20: A theme: Naomi asks to be called Mara (“bitter”), “for the Almighty has dealt very bitterly with me.” The name-change marks the distance between who she was and what she has become. The gospel reverses name-changes in the other direction: Abram → Abraham, Simon → Peter. Those who come to Christ bearing names given by affliction receive a new name written by the redeemer himself (Revelation 2:17; Ruth 3:12).
21: A revelation of God: “I went out full, and the LORD has brought me back empty.” YHWH’s answer to Naomi’s emptiness is already prepared: the narrative has planted Boaz in position (1:22 brings Ruth to Bethlehem at the barley harvest). YHWH’s response to “I am empty” is already in place before the prayer is voiced (Isaiah 65:24: “Before they call I will answer”).
22: A theme: they came to Bethlehem “at the beginning of the barley harvest.” The arrival at harvest time is the book’s first hint that emptiness will become fullness. Christ uses harvest as his primary image for the gathering of those who belong to him (Matthew 9:37-38). The grain that falls and dies bears much fruit (John 12:24). The whole harvest economy Boaz presides over is the economic form of the gospel’s abundance.
